Cooperation is another effective activator of liking. Shared interests and mutual goals promote cooperation, which in turn promotes liking. Despite the research evidence that cooperation produces liking, additional research shows that people are typically poor at identifying the shared interests and goals that foster cooperation. Professor Lee Thompson, for example, analyzed 32 negotiation research studies involving more than 5,000 participants to discover if bargainers were good at uncovering shared interests within negotiations. The results were striking. Negotiators failed to identify shared interests and goals that were genuinely present in the situation about 50 percent of the time. This kind of error can seriously undermine bargaining success. For instance, a separate study followed professional labor and contract negotiators over nine years and rated them as average or skilled at obtaining the desired outcome. Those rated as average spent only 10 percent of their time seeking out shared interests and goals and goals with their counterparts, devoting the rest of their time to staking out and defending their own positions. Compared to the average negotiators, the more successful skilled negotiators spent four times as much effort searching for complementary interests and goals before moving on to stating their own positions. It was that difference that spurred cooperation that made them top negotiators.
